q: State Epictetus's core principle about what is and is not 'up to us', with a verbatim quote from the knowledge base in the source language.

judge.note: Answer clearly states the up-to-us/not-up-to-us division but does not explicitly include the full list of items (e.g., desire/aversion) nor the nature of power as free/unhindered vs weak/slavish, and the quote is incomplete rather than verbatim full core principle.
judge.raw: {"facts_covered":[1,2],"note":"Answer clearly states the up-to-us/not-up-to-us division but does not explicitly include the full list of items (e.g., desire/aversion) nor the nature of power as free/unhindered vs weak/slavish, and the quote is incomplete rather than verbatim full core principle."}

misattributions.claim: The quote 'Of things some are in our power, and others are not...' is given as Epictetus’s verbatim knowledge-base quote.
“то единственное «в нашей власти» и есть προαίρεσις” means “the only thing ‘in our power’ is prohairesis (choice/judgment).

confusion.note: No clear confusion between two specific thinkers; however the answer attributes retrieved verbatim Russian/English passages to Epictetus without the key specifying that attribution.

trace.final_text:
Epictetus’s core principle is a strict division between what is “up to us” and what is not: only our judgment/choice is in our power, while external things are not.

Verbatim quote from the knowledge base:
> “Of things some are in our power, and others are not. In our power are...” 

The retrieved material also states, in Russian, the same core idea:
> “то единственное «в нашей власти» и есть προαίρεσις”

That is, “the only thing ‘in our power’ is prohairesis” (choice/judgment).

final:

The retrieved material also states, in Russian, the same core idea:

That is, “the only thing ‘in our power’ is prohairesis” (choice/judgment).
